sourceText: He expelled Afghan, Ottoman, and Russian invaders, ending a period of chaos and reunifying the nation under his rule.
Military Modernization
He built a powerful, modern standing army and navy, making Persia the dominant military power in the region.
Invasion of India
His successful invasion of the Mughal Empire and sack of Delhi brought immense wealth, including the Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor diamond, to Persia.
Why is Nader Shah called the 'Napoleon of Persia'?
He is compared to Napoleon for his brilliant military tactics, his rise from a modest background to become an emperor, and his vast conquests.
What led to his downfall?

His reign grew increasingly tyrannical and paranoid, especially after an assassination attempt. His cruelty and crushing taxes led to widespread rebellion and his eventual assassination by his own commanders.
What is his most famous loot from India?
He plundered the Mughal treasury in Delhi, taking legendary treasures like the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor and Daria-i-Noor diamonds.
Born in Tekirdağ, Ottoman Empire.
Joins the secret society of the Young Ottomans.
Flees to London and Paris, co-publishes the newspaper Hürriyet (Freedom).
Stages his play Vatan Yahut Silistre (Fatherland or Silistra), leading to his exile in Famagusta, Cyprus.
The first Ottoman Constitution is declared; Kemal serves on the drafting committee.
Sultan Abdülhamid II suspends the constitution and exiles Kemal again.
Dies in exile on the island of Chios.
Founding Member of the Young Ottomans
He was a leading intellectual force in the Young Ottomans movement, which advocated for a constitutional monarchy and modernization.
Pioneering Turkish Playwright and Novelist
Authored the influential play 'Vatan Yahut Silistre' and the first modern Turkish novel, 'İntibah' (Awakening).
Champion of 'Fatherland' and 'Freedom'
He popularized the concepts of 'vatan' (fatherland) and 'hürriyet' (freedom), laying the ideological groundwork for modern Turkish nationalism.
What were the Young Ottomans trying to achieve?

The Young Ottomans were a secret society of intellectuals who wanted to reform the Ottoman Empire. They sought to blend Western democratic ideas, like a constitution and parliament, with Islamic principles to create a modern, unified Ottoman identity and resist European encroachment.
Why was his play 'Vatan Yahut Silistre' so controversial?

The play, depicting the heroic defense of a fortress during the Crimean War, ignited intense patriotic feelings in the audience. The public demonstrations that followed were seen by the government as a direct challenge to the Sultan's authority, leading to Kemal's arrest and exile.
What is Namık Kemal's legacy in modern Turkey?

He is revered as a national hero and the 'Poet of the Fatherland.' His ideas on constitutionalism, patriotism, and freedom heavily influenced the Young Turk movement and, ultimately, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, the founder of the Republic of Turkey.
Born in Tus, Khorasan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Takes refuge with the Nizari Isma'ilis (Assassins), eventually being held in Alamut Castle.
Alamut Castle falls to the Mongols. Tusi enters the service of the Mongol ruler Hulagu Khan.
Construction begins on the Maragheh observatory under Tusi's direction.
Completes his major work on ethics, Akhlaq-i Nasiri (The Nasirean Ethics).
The Maragheh observatory team completes the highly influential astronomical tables, the Zij-i Ilkhani.
Dies in Kadhimiya, near Baghdad, and is buried in a shrine next to the seventh Twelver Shia Imam.
Maragheh Observatory
Founded and directed the most advanced astronomical observatory of its time, attracting top scholars from across the known world.
The Tusi Couple
A mathematical theorem that generates linear motion from the sum of two circular motions, which was later used by Copernicus.
Zij-i Ilkhani (Ilkhanic Tables)
A highly accurate set of astronomical tables that charted planetary movements, becoming a standard reference for centuries.
Akhlaq-i Nasiri (Nasirean Ethics)
A seminal work on ethics that synthesized Aristotelian, Platonic, and Islamic thought, influencing Islamic philosophy for generations.
Was Tusi really a prisoner of the Assassins?

He initially sought refuge with them but was later prevented from leaving, effectively making him a prisoner. However, he was treated as a scholar, not a common captive, and was able to continue his scientific and philosophical work.
How did Tusi influence Copernicus?

Tusi's mathematical model, the 'Tusi couple,' appears in Copernicus's major work, De revolutionibus. It is used to replace Ptolemy's equant. It is debated whether Copernicus learned of it through Byzantine or Arabic manuscripts, but the mathematical similarity is undeniable.
What fields did Tusi contribute to?

He was a true polymath who made significant contributions to astronomy, mathematics (especially trigonometry), philosophy, ethics, logic, optics, medicine, and theology. He wrote over 150 works in his lifetime.
What was his role in the Mongol invasion?

When the Mongols besieged the Assassin fortress of Alamut, Tusi helped negotiate a peaceful surrender. His knowledge and wisdom impressed the Mongol leader Hulagu Khan, who then appointed him as a scientific advisor, saving his life and enabling his greatest achievements.
c. 634 BCE
Birth of Nebuchadnezzar II.
605 BCE
Defeats Egyptians at the Battle of Carchemish; ascends to the throne of Babylon.
597 BCE
First siege of Jerusalem. Deports King Jehoiachin and other elites to Babylon.
Second siege of Jerusalem. Destroys the city and Solomon's Temple, beginning the Babylonian Captivity.
Conducts a 13-year siege of the city of Tyre.
Reigns as king, overseeing massive construction projects in Babylon, including the Ishtar Gate.
c. 562 BCE
Dies in Babylon, succeeded by his son Amel-Marduk.
Conquest of Judah and Destruction of Jerusalem
He crushed the Kingdom of Judah, destroying Solomon's Temple and deporting its populace, a pivotal event in Jewish history.
Rebuilding of Babylon
He transformed Babylon into the largest and most magnificent city of its time, constructing the Ishtar Gate and the Etemenanki ziggurat.
Imperial Expansion
He secured the Neo-Babylonian Empire's dominance over the Near East after defeating Egyptian and Assyrian forces at Carchemish.
Did Nebuchadnezzar really build the Hanging Gardens of Babylon?

While ancient writers like Berossus credit him with building the Hanging Gardens for his wife, there is no definitive archaeological evidence or contemporary Babylonian text that confirms their existence. Their story remains a legendary part of his reign.
Why is he so prominent in the Bible?

He is a central antagonist in the Old Testament for conquering Jerusalem, destroying the First Temple, and forcing the Jews into the Babylonian Captivity. The Book of Daniel also features him prominently, detailing his interactions with the prophet Daniel and his supposed period of madness.
What was his relationship with the god Marduk?

As king of Babylon, Nebuchadnezzar was the chief servant of Marduk, the patron god of the city. His inscriptions frequently credit Marduk for his victories and describe his extensive temple-building projects as acts of piety to honor the god.
Born Abu Ali Hasan ibn Ali Tusi in Tus, Persia.
Appointed as vizier to the Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan.
Secures the throne for Malik-Shah I after Alp Arslan's assassination, becoming the empire's most powerful figure.
Founds the Nizamiyyah schools across the empire, with the most famous one in Baghdad established in 1065.
Writes the Siyasatnama (Book of Government), a major work on statecraft.
Assassinated near Nahavand, Persia, believed to be by a member of the Order of Assassins.
Centralization of the Seljuk Empire
He created a powerful centralized bureaucracy that unified the vast and diverse territories of the Seljuk Empire.
Founder of the Nizamiyyahs
He established a network of state-sponsored universities (Nizamiyyahs) that became premier institutions of learning and administration.
Author of Siyasatnama
He wrote the 'Book of Government' (Siyasatnama), a foundational text on political theory and statecraft in the Islamic world.
What were the Nizamiyyah schools?
They were a system of higher education institutions founded by Nizam al-Mulk. They promoted Sunni Islamic theology and law, trained future government officials, and countered rival ideologies.
Why was Nizam al-Mulk assassinated?

While the Order of Assassins (Hashshashin) is officially blamed, his immense power had created many enemies at court, including rival ministers and members of the royal family, who may have been complicit.
What is the 'Siyasatnama' about?

The 'Siyasatnama' or 'Book of Government' is a treatise on kingship and statecraft. It offers advice to rulers on justice, administration, court etiquette, and dealing with internal and external threats.
c. 550 BCE
Birth of Darius.
522 BCE
Seizes the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after overthrowing the usurper Gaumata.
Suppresses widespread revolts across the empire.
c. 519 BCE
Begins work on the Behistun Inscription to legitimize his rule.
c. 515 BCE
Begins construction of the new ceremonial capital, Persepolis.
c. 513 BCE
Launches an unsuccessful campaign against the Scythians in Europe.
499 BCE
The Ionian Revolt begins, marking the start of the Greco-Persian Wars.
490 BCE
The first Persian invasion of Greece ends in defeat at the Battle of Marathon.
486 BCE
Dies in Persia while preparing a second expedition against Greece.
Imperial Reorganization (Satrapy System)
Divided the empire into provinces (satrapies), each governed by officials who reported directly to him, ensuring centralized control.
The Royal Road
Constructed a 2,700 km highway from Susa to Sardis, enabling rapid communication and troop movement via a relay of postal stations.
Standardized Coinage and Weights
Introduced the gold daric and silver siglos, creating a unified economic system across the vast empire.
Construction of Persepolis
Initiated the building of the grand ceremonial capital, showcasing the wealth, power, and multicultural nature of the Achaemenid Empire.
Was Darius related to Cyrus the Great?
Distantly. Darius belonged to a collateral branch of the Achaemenid dynasty. He solidified his claim by marrying Atossa, the daughter of Cyrus the Great.
What was the Behistun Inscription?
A massive multilingual inscription carved on a cliff, detailing Darius's rise to power. It was a key piece of royal propaganda and was crucial for the modern decipherment of cuneiform script.
Did Darius conquer Greece?
No. His first invasion was famously defeated at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE. He died before he could launch a second, much larger invasion, which was left to his son, Xerxes I.
